name: Hindu Cosmology
connections: Samsara - cycle of rebirth driven by karma
Dharma - righteous duty related to karma
Svarga - heavenly realms earned through positive karma
Naraka - hellish realms as karmic punishment

richMetadata.structure: Karma operates as a self-executing universal law of moral causality across infinite timescales and rebirths. Actions generate impressions (samskaras) that shape future circumstances, destiny, and rebirth placement. Three types: Sanchita (accumulated), Prarabdha (ripening), Kriyamana (new).
connections: Samsara - eternal cycle driven by karma
Bhagavad Gita - Krishna's teachings on karma yoga
richMetadata.significance: Karma embodies personal responsibility and justice. It explains suffering as consequence of past actions rather than divine punishment. Offers hope through the possibility of improving one's situation through right action. Central to Hindu ethics and understanding of universal order (Rta).
parallels: Islamic divine justice and human choice

deities.name: All sentient beings subject to karma

keyMyths.title: The Churning of the Ocean of Milk (Samudra Manthan)
keyMyths.description: Devas (gods) and Asuras (demons) cooperate to churn the cosmic ocean to obtain Amrita (the elixir of immortality). This event results in the emergence of various celestial beings, treasures, and the poison Halahala, which Shiva consumes to save the universe.

keyMyths.title: The Cosmic Egg (Brahmanda)
keyMyths.description: The universe originates from a golden egg (Hiranyagarbha) or Brahmanda, which contains all the elements and potential for creation. Brahma, the creator god, emerges from this egg and divides it to form heaven and earth.

keyMyths.title: The Trimurti and Cosmic Cycles (Yugas)
keyMyths.description: The Trimurti – Brahma, Vishnu, and Shiva – represent creation, preservation, and destruction, respectively. They govern the cyclical nature of time through Yugas (epochs) characterized by decreasing dharma (righteousness).

keyMyths.title: Mount Meru as the Axis Mundi
keyMyths.description: Mount Meru is considered the central mountain of the universe, serving as the axis mundi or cosmic center. The sun, moon, and stars revolve around it, and it is the abode of the gods and celestial beings.

keyMyths.title: The Avatars of Vishnu
keyMyths.description: Vishnu descends to earth in various avatars (incarnations) to restore dharma and defeat evil. These avatars, such as Rama and Krishna, play crucial roles in maintaining cosmic balance and order.

sources.name: Rigveda
sources.description: Ancient collection of Vedic Sanskrit hymns, containing early cosmological ideas.
sources.title: Rigveda

sources.name: Bhagavata Purana
sources.description: A prominent Purana that elaborates on Hindu cosmology and mythology.
sources.title: Bhagavata Purana

sources.name: Vishnu Purana
sources.description: One of the eighteen major Puranas, detailing the creation and history of the universe from a Vaishnava perspective.
sources.title: Vishnu Purana

sources.name: Wendy Doniger O'Flaherty, *Hindu Myths*
sources.description: A comprehensive collection and analysis of Hindu myths.
sources.title: Wendy Doniger O'Flaherty, *Hindu Myths*

sources.name: Heinrich Zimmer, *Myths and Symbols in Indian Art and Civilization*
sources.description: An exploration of the symbolic meaning of Hindu myths and their representation in art.
sources.title: Heinrich Zimmer, *Myths and Symbols in Indian Art and Civilization*
